desc: Muscat is one of the Gulf's calmer capitals: low-rise, sea-facing and mountain-backed, with whitewashed districts, a striking grand mosque, a long Mutrah waterfront and easy escapes to wadis, forts and desert-edge scenery. It works best for travellers who want winter sun and culture without the vertical-city intensity of Dubai or Doha.

todo.name: Sultan Qaboos Grand Mosque
todo.desc: Visit outside prayer times and dress appropriately for one of Muscat's signature architectural sights.

todo.name: Mutrah Souq and Corniche
todo.desc: Walk the waterfront, then dive into incense, textile and silver lanes in the souq.

todo.name: Royal Opera House area
todo.desc: Check performances or simply use the district for polished modern Omani design.

todo.name: Wadi Shab or Wadi Bani Khalid trip
todo.desc: Plan a full day with swimming gear and careful heat timing for a wadi excursion.

todo.name: Old Muscat and Al Alam Palace area
todo.desc: Use the older ceremonial district to see how Muscat shifts from souq bustle to clipped, official sea-facing grandeur.

todo.name: Sunset on Mutrah Fort side
todo.desc: Climb above the corniche late in the day for the best look at the harbour curve and the city's softer evening light.

wifi.note: Usually excellent in city hotels, malls and cafe districts, but once you head into wadis, beach coves or mountain roads you should not assume reliable coverage.

prac.fltNote: Muscat International is close enough to keep arrivals easy, but the relevant UK reality is Heathrow and hub connections rather than LGW or LCY. Oman Air is currently selling Muscat-London Heathrow flights, so destination-level direct flags stay false only because the UI tracks Gatwick and City. Winter sun demand from late November into March is when fares and better hotels tend to tighten fastest.
prac.lang: Arabic; English is common in hotels and transport
prac.visa: British citizens can visit Oman for up to 14 days without a visa, and GOV.UK says your passport must be valid for at least 6 months after arrival. If you want to stay longer, it is better to arrange the appropriate visa online before travel, and border officials may ask for onward travel, accommodation proof and travel health insurance.
alerts: Summer heat can be extreme and limits outdoor sightseeing.
Dress codes matter at mosques and conservative public spaces.
Friday timings and prayer hours can reshape sightseeing plans more than first-time visitors expect.
Wadi trips can be flash-flood sensitive after rain.

sweet: December to March is usually Muscat's strongest stretch, with genuinely pleasant walking weather, sea-facing evenings that feel good rather than punishing, and the cleanest conditions for mosque, corniche and wadi days.
